howItWorks.intro: This calculator compares the total cost of ownership between an electric vehicle and a gas car, including fuel costs, maintenance savings, and purchase price difference.
rules: Gas fuel cost = (annual miles / MPG) × gas price per gallon
EV fuel cost = (annual miles / EV efficiency) × electricity rate per kWh
Break-even point = price difference / (annual gas cost − annual EV cost + maintenance savings)
CO₂ savings based on EPA average of 19.6 lbs CO₂ per gallon of gas burned
howItWorks.outro: EVs typically save $800–$1,500/year in fuel and $500+/year in maintenance. However, the higher purchase price means it may take 3–7 years to break even. Consider available tax credits ($7,500 federal) which can dramatically shorten the payback period.

faq.question: How does Oregon handle this differently?
faq.answer: Oregon has its own specific rules, rates, and limits that may differ significantly from federal guidelines or other states. This calculator uses Oregon-specific data where available. Always verify with a local professional for important decisions.

faq.question: How much cheaper is it to fuel an EV vs a gas car?
faq.answer: On average, EV drivers spend 60-70% less on fuel. At national averages ($3.50/gal gas, $0.14/kWh electricity), driving 12,000 miles costs about $1,500 in gas but only $480 in electricity. Exact savings depend on local gas and electricity prices.

faq.question: What is a good EV efficiency rating?
faq.answer: Most modern EVs achieve 3-4 miles per kWh. Efficient models like the Tesla Model 3 and Hyundai Ioniq 6 get 4+ mi/kWh, while larger EVs like trucks may get 2-2.5 mi/kWh. The default 3.5 mi/kWh represents a typical mid-size EV.

faq.question: How long does it take for an EV to pay for itself?
faq.answer: Typically 4-7 years when comparing similar vehicle classes. If the EV costs $6,000 more and saves $1,500/year in fuel plus $500 in maintenance, break-even is about 3 years. Federal and state tax credits can reduce this to under 2 years.

faq.question: Do EVs really save on maintenance?
faq.answer: Yes. EVs have no oil changes, transmission fluid, spark plugs, or timing belts. Brake pads last 2-3x longer due to regenerative braking. Average EV maintenance is $0.03/mile vs $0.06/mile for gas cars — roughly $400/year savings on 12,000 miles.

faq.question: How much CO₂ does switching to an EV save?
faq.answer: A gas car averaging 28 MPG emits about 8,400 lbs of CO₂ per 12,000 miles. Even accounting for power plant emissions from charging, an EV produces roughly 50-70% less CO₂. In areas with clean grids (hydro, solar, wind), savings are even greater.

faq.question: Does home charging vs public charging affect the comparison?
faq.answer: Significantly. Home charging at off-peak rates ($0.08-0.12/kWh) is cheapest. DC fast charging stations charge $0.30-0.60/kWh, which cuts into savings. If you rely entirely on fast charging, your fuel savings vs gas drop from ~70% to ~30-40%.

chart.subtitle: Assumes $3.50/gal, 28 MPG, $0.14/kWh, 3.5 mi/kWh. Includes estimated maintenance difference.
